data.title: AMZY: Collecting Income Where It Isn't Usually Possible
data.description:
Overview

Many of the YieldMax ETFs offer such massive distribution yields that they are difficult to pass up. The general rule of thumb that I follow is that if I held an underlying position in the common shares of a company that one of the YieldMax ETFs aimed to track, I would initiate a small position. Since I held shares of Amazon (AMZN) at the time, I also started a position in YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:AMZY). AMZY offers a very large annual distribution rate of more than 65%, supported by a synthetic option strategy. A large distribution rate like this comes with some unconventional benefits and risks that investors should consider.

I previously covered AMZY and issued a buy rating. Since my last coverage, we've seen some diverse market conditions, which provides us with a little insight into the fund's potential. Following the recent market volatility, I wanted to revisit this option ETF to provide some updated thoughts and insights into its utility and appeal going forward. Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that the price has suffered from a severe decline of 31.6%. When including all distributions that were paid over the same time frame, the total return jumps up to 7.4%.

So, is the high yield offered worth the higher risk profile? I, personally, believe the risk can be worth it, but there are some caveats to that statement. At first glance, there are some clear risks that you can probably spot. However, there are some benefits that a fund like AMZY offers that may not be as obvious if you are looking at this ETF through a traditional lens. AMZY has a gross expense ratio of 0.99% and has net assets of approximately $256M.

Fund Strategy

AMZY deploys a synthetic option strategy with two goals in mind. The first is to replicate the daily price movement of Amazon and the second goal is to harness the implied volatility levels in order to generate option premiums. These option premiums are what funds all of the distributions paid out by the fund. However, the term 'synthetic' means that AMZY doesn't actually own any of the underlying shares of Amazon.

Looking at the holdings below, we can see that AMZY holds some US Treasuries and active call options. This can significantly increase the risk profile of the fund in terms of volatility and price stability. Since AMZY doesn't actually hold shares of Amazon, there can be a large price return discrepancy between the two.

In order to simulate the daily price movement of Amazon, AMZY simultaneously buys call options and sells put options. AMZY implements OTM (out-of-the-money) options and typically selects a strike price that is set between 5% to 15% above the current market price of Amazon. The inclusion of an option strategy naturally limits the upside price potential up to the selected strike price. The fund's prospectus clearly lays out the rules and strategy around the option strategy that I would suggest reading through for further clarity.

Just to demonstrate the vulnerability of the strategy, we can see how AMZY's price compared to AMZN's price over the last six months. When looking at the price movement of the two lined up against one another, it tells a story of the overall vulnerability of the fund. Since the upside is capped due to the option strategy, AMZY is only capable of partially participating in upside movement.

However, we can see how AMZY experiences a more dramatic downside whenever market momentum is negative. Most recently, markets declined in the first quarter of 2025 due to President Trump's tariff proposals. Both AMZY and AMZN declined, but AMZN's price rapidly recovered over recent weeks. Since AMZY's upside is capped, the ETF is unlikely to ever experience the same sort of recovery over time.

Risk and Comparison

The severe price downside that can be experienced is ultimately the main risk of a long-term position in AMZY. Since YieldMax has launched so many different option ETFs that use a similar strategy, there are tons of examples to pull from. Many of these YieldMax funds have shared a similar fate of the price and NAV deteriorating over time, which reinforces that idea that there are inherent tradeoffs when a fund pays a distribution rate this large. Here are some of the most popular YieldMax funds and the respective equities they aim to replicate the movement of.

  • YieldMax MSFT Option Income ETF (MSFO) - Microsoft (MSFT)
  • YieldMax COIN Option Income ETF (CONY) - Coinbase (COIN)
  • YieldMax TSLA Option Income ETF (TSLY) - Tesla (TSLA)
  • YieldMax AMD Option Income ETF (AMDY) - Advanced Micro Devices (AMD)
  • YieldMax META Option Income ETF (FBY) - Meta Platforms (META)
  • YieldMax NVDA Option Income ETF (NVDY) - Nvidia (NVDA)
  • YieldMax MSTR Option Income ETF (MSTY) - Strategy (MSTR)
  • YieldMax NFLX Option Income ETF (NFLY) - Netflix (NFLX)

The performance of these option ETFs is obviously tied to the fact that each tracks a different underlying asset. As a result, MSTY and NFLY are the two outliers here that still managed to provide a positive price return over the last year. Seeing that some of these funds experienced declines greater than 50%, investors should question whether or not a risk of this kind is worth the income potential.

The possibility for large declines like this, is why specifically stated that YieldMax funds are best utilized as accompanying positions. These option ETFs will not be the most efficient way to achieve the highest total return possible. There are many instances where a YieldMax fund has underperformed the very same asset that it aims to track. However, AMZY has done a great job at providing a similar total return that stays on par with AMZN since the ETF's inception.

Based on the performance and strategy of AMZY so far, here are some possible outcomes that will happen based on the future of AMZN.

  1. If the share price of Amazon rapidly increases, AMZY will likely underperform, since the upside potential is capped.
  2. If Amazon trades sideways or sees slower growth, there is a potential for AMZY to outperform. The combination of capital appreciation and high distributions can lead to more favorable results.
  3. If Amazon slowly declines over time, so will AMZY's price. However, there is the potential for AMZY's income to offset the price declines. The caveat is that a lower price and net asset value may also result in lower distribution totals.
  4. As most recently experienced, if Amazon's price rapidly decreases, AMZY's price will as well. The capped upside will make it difficult and unlikely for AMZY to be able to reach the prior price levels before the decline.

Strategic Dividend Goal

Despite these risks, I still maintain optimistic about the utility of the ETF. With many of these YieldMax ETFs that offer a distribution rate over 50%, I believe that the end goal should be to reach 'house money' status. I classify house money status as the cross point where the distributions received are equivalent to or outpace the amount of capital initially invested. While this is unconventional, it's certainly a possibility. For instance, AMZY went public at around $20 per share. Since then, here is the total amount of distributions that have been paid out:

  • 2023: $2.1178 per share paid out in distributions.
  • 2024: $9.0116 per share paid out in distributions.
  • 2025 YTD: $2.6502 per share paid out in distributions
  • Grand Total: $13.7796 per share paid out in distribution since inception.

So if you invested $10,000 into AMZY at the time of inception, which is equivalent to ~500 shares, you would have now received ~$6,889 back in distributions. Since 2024 was the only full year that we can reference, we can estimate that the actual annual distribution rate sits around 45%. From this perspective, we can assume that an investor can get their initial invested capital back in about two and a half years. Investors should be aware that just because the math makes sense, there are still risks that can change the pace of AMZY's returns and overall appeal.

For instance, the dividend payouts can vary month to month and are dependent on market conditions and the overall success of the underlying option strategy. The general trend seems to be that AMZY's distributions are a lot lower when Amazon's share price is trending downward. We can see below that the payouts in the first quarter of 2025 were lower than their usual levels. Coincidentally, this is when market indexes were in decline.

Another part of the appeal of AMZY's large distributions is the tax-efficiency. AMZY strategically issues out ROC (return of capital) distributions, which aren't technically considered income. ROC is generally when a fund gives you back your own capital in the form of dividend, rather than cash that was earned from the underlying business or fund. ROC distributions don't have any income taxes associated, but they do reduce an investor's cost basis. So if you have an average cost basis of $10 and a fund pays a $1 ROC distribution, your cost basis is now reduced down to $9.

If you maintain a position long enough, you can technically reach a point where the cost basis is reduced down to $0 per share. At that point, all distributions received will be classified as long term capital gains. Looking at the most recent payout for May, nearly 95% of the distribution was made up of ROC. Therefore, there's the potential that AMZY can be efficiently utilized within a brokerage account with very little tax consequences.

I am fairly optimistic about the future of Amazon, which is why I continue to maintain a long-term position. My optimism has become stronger since President Trump exempted tech from reciprocal tariffs as well. I believe that one of the most anticipated drivers of growth over the next decade will be the Artificial Intelligence innovations that Amazon continues to build on and invest in.

The main source of involvement is through different channels within AWS (Amazon Web Services), which helps other companies build and expand upon their own AI tools. Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034 and Amazon is uniquely positioned to participate in the growth of the market. Amazon currently earns a strong buy rating from both Seeking Alpha's Quant system and Wall Street analysts. However, this growth doesn't happen without its fair share of speed bumps and barriers. For instance, we already saw how markets negatively reacted at the start of the year when DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) launched a similar service to ChatGPT at a fraction of the cost. AMZY can harness these moments of volatility driven by doubt and reward shareholders over the next decade.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on AMZY as the fund has proven to deliver consistent distributions throughout different market environments. Although the synthetic strategy comes with its own set of risks and tradeoffs, there are still plenty of benefits that income investors may be able to enjoy. For instance, early investors of AMZY are well on their way to achieving 'house money', which can be a unique and unconventional approach to these YieldMax funds. I believe that AMZY is best utilized as an accompanying fund, rather than a core position because of the high probability of underperforming over a longer time horizon.

data.title: Endava: Priced For Stagnation Or Obsolescence
data.description:
Introduction

I recently updated my view on Globant (GLOB), which saw its stock crash 30% after a modest miss and lowered guidance. This resembles what has happened to Endava (NYSE:DAVA), and I decided to take a deeper look at this company's operations and valuation. As in many cases for IT consultants, it seems that DAVA has been hit by a buyer's strike first due to AI, which caused clients/companies to stop and reassess how to spend IT budgets and now the Trump tariff tantrum that threw many companies into crisis mode and placed IT decision on the back burner or in the trash can.

The Growth Challenge

The company experienced a sharp decline in revenue starting in 2Q23, sharply contrasting with its more than 20-year history. This was due to AI technology breakthroughs that forced many companies to reassess how to implement AI, what to do with current IT projects, who and how much to budget for new contracts. This buyer's strike lasted most of 2023. It was starting to see trend normalization when the tariff crisis hit and threw companies into crisis mode, causing delays in new contracts or cancellations of existing ones.

DAVA, Globant, EPAM (EPAM), etc. have highly stable business models, where contracts provide visibility for revenue and earnings. The AI and now tariff crisis threw a massive wrench into this visibility and forced companies to downgrade guidance to what current contracts are slated to generate revenue. New contracts, i.e., growth, were taken off the table.

In 1Q25 (fiscal 3Q25), DAVA reported zero revenue growth compared to 1Q24 but posted a sharp gain in margins, which was due to reduced headcount or productivity. AI has been a positive factor for IT consultants; they can utilize technology to write code, etc.

When Can Growth Resume

Looking at the consensus quarterly revenue forecast, the market has implemented DAVA's guidance and assumes almost flat revenue for the remainder of calendar 2025. Green shoots are expected in 2Q26, and a new top-line growth expectation of under 10% for fiscal 2027 is also expected.

While these revenue estimates seem low, the company has demonstrated that it can maintain or improve margins, which leads to an EBITDA growth of 100% in 2025 and around 10% in the 2026/27 time frame. My preferred metric, cash earnings or CE (net income, depreciation + stock-based compensation), may grow around 11%. The bar has been set very low for DAVA.

More importantly, with valuation severely impacted, they will be unlikely to execute significant acquisitions, resulting in over US$100m in free cash flow that can shore up the balance sheet or be used to buy back shares.

Valuation: Priced for Stagnation

This is the good part of DAVA. The stock has fallen hard since 2024, down 80%, leaving it at a deep discount of 6.2x P/CE or 0.5x PEG on fiscal 2025 estimates. The market has thrown in the towel and expects the company to stagnate or shrink into obscurity. The risk-reward equation is compelling. If DAVA can show modest revenue and earnings growth in the next few quarters, the stock can recover.

The consensus price target for fiscal 2026 (June 2026) is US$25 or 10x P/CE at a .9x PEG. I agree that DAVA does not warrant a higher multiple yet and value the stock at 1x PEG or US$27 for FY26, which is over 90% upside potential.

Peer Comps

I compiled a comparison table using consensus data that measures growth and valuation for a wide range of peers and competitors. DAVA stands out as one of the cheapest on a relative or PEG metric. The large caps look expensive, and most suffer the same AI and tariff hits.

Risk

The sector is highly competitive, and in an economic slowdown, some companies may elect to price services/contracts lower, which may impact Endava's current and future margins as well as growth.

Conclusion

I rate DAVA a buy. The stock has been deeply discounted to the point that it incorporates no growth or obsolescence, providing a compelling risk-reward equation. The first sign of revenue/contract growth may start a rally with 90% upside potential.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: Asset Divestitures May Raise Over $3 Billion, Triggering A Valuation Re-Rating
data.description:
The Kraft Heinz Company (NASDAQ:KHC), unless you invested some decades ago, would have disappointed you in the past five years. The dividend yield has been acceptable (almost 6% today), but the stock has continued its slow and steady downward spiral. So far this year, KHC stock is down about 10%. Amid ongoing business struggles (net sales expected to decline around 2.5% this year), Kraft Heinz yesterday announced that it is considering strategic transactions to unlock shareholder value. The company also warned that this evaluation may not necessarily result in any transaction. For what it's worth, this is a vague announcement, but what we can infer is that Kraft Heinz is trying to secure deals to dispose of some assets/brands that have not been performing well in the recent past.

The objective of this analysis is to understand the strategic actions the company may pursue to drive long-term shareholder returns higher, and then to quantify the financial impact of such decisions. Later in this analysis, you will find some estimates that I made to understand the financial impact of asset divestitures.

The Core Portfolio is Exposed to Secular Unfavorable Trends

Before delving deeper to make assumptions about Kraft Heinz's strategic moves, we need to understand the main reasons behind the company's decline. I will keep it brief here.

The first problem is that Kraft Heinz's core portfolio, which includes the most iconic brands it owns, is unfavorably exposed to the growing healthy, clean eating habits. According to Innova Market Insights, nearly 1 in 2 consumers have purchased more fresh, unprocessed food over the past year (as of March) compared to the previous year. The rise of clean, healthy eating is clear. The Covid-19 pandemic has accelerated this trend, in my opinion. Many of Kraft Heinz's iconic brands, including Oscar Mayer, Kraft Singles, Kraft Macaroni & Cheese, and Lunchables thrive on processed food. This does not bode well with the changing consumer behavior. Persistent organic sales declines confirm the company's struggles.

Exhibit 1: Q1 2025 Organic Sales Growth

Kraft Heinz's core portfolio is also exposed to the war against sugar as products such as Capri Sun, Jell-O, and even the iconic Heinz Ketchup line up contain high sugar content. The high sodium content in products such as canned soups and processed meats has not helped the company's story either.

The second problem is the growth of private label brands, especially from retail giants such as Walmart Inc. (WMT), Costco Wholesale (COST), and Kroger (KR). The problem is that these private-label products are cheaper compared to Kraft Heinz's. A recent report by eMarketer highlighted how private label growth has eclipsed that of national brands in the post-pandemic era.

Exhibit 2: Private Label Growth vs. National Brands

Impairment charges to popular brands such as Lunchables, Oscar Mayer, and Kraft natural cheese confirm that the company's core portfolio has been hit hard.

There has been a lot going wrong for Kraft Heinz, of course, including questionable marketing budget cuts that led to an erosion in brand equity before Miguel Patricio stepped in to steer the company in the right direction. Anyways, the two reasons discussed above, in my opinion, are at the heart of its problems.

What Might Strategic Transactions Entail?

Yesterday's announcement is vague, but for good reason, I believe. This leaves the company with a lot of options to explore. There are a few types of transactions that come to my mind when I read the press release.

  1. Brand divestitures.
  2. Acquisitions.
  3. Spin-off of certain business units.

Although acquisitions can be on the table, given the company's debt burden, Kraft Heinz will have to focus on faster-growing categories that are likely to hedge against the overall decline in volume. Think of categories like healthy snacks and ethnic foods. Acquisitions are unlikely to be a big part of the strategic transactions the company is thinking about right now.

Spin-offs might work if a specific business segment is seemingly undervalued. This is an avenue the company might explore. Again, I believe spin-offs will not be a major part of any strategic transactions in the foreseeable future.

That brings us to asset divestitures.

This is the more likely path forward for Kraft Heinz at this point. The company has a history of offloading underperforming assets, which includes the likes of selling the Planters brand to Hormel Foods (HRL) for $3.35 billion in February 2021 and selling the natural cheese business for $3.3 billion to Groupe Lactalis in late 2021. If Kraft Heinz were to follow the same path this time around, the rationale is likely to be realigning the brand portfolio with the company's longer-term vision, which can be achieved by retaining brands with higher margins and better growth profiles.

My next task is to hypothesize which business units may be considered for divestiture if Kraft Heinz decides to go ahead with such a plan. As close followers of the company may know, Kraft Heinz has assigned different categories for different business units. All products fall under one of the following categories.

  1. Accelerate
  2. Protect
  3. Balance

Since the Balance platform is home to commodity-heavy, flat-growth products that are expected to help Kraft Heinz maintain its brand footprint, brands that are categorized under this platform may naturally come under the chopping block if the company decides to look for asset divestitures. After considering this aspect and recent impairments, I believe below business lines are likely to be considered for strategic transactions in the future.

  • The meat platform, which includes Oscar Mayer, which reported a $1.3 billion impairment in Q4 2024. The broader Meat & Cheese platform itself reported a goodwill impairment of $854 million in Q2 2024, which suggests the meat platform is not as valuable to the company as previously thought. The meat platform also suffers from the challenges faced by the processed food industry, which I discussed earlier, which could be another incentive for Kraft Heinz to consider divesting these assets.
  • The coffee platform, which includes Maxwell House, may also be a target. The Canada and North America Coffee unit reported impairment charges in both 2023 and 2022, and the excess fair value of this business unit is less than 10% of its carrying value. The coffee platform has suffered at the hands of private labels in the past five years.
  • Certain parts of the cheese platform may also come under scrutiny in a fresh evaluation of business prospects.
  • Lunchables, which is a segment that is reported within the Substantial Snacking platform, is also likely to be scrutinized, in my opinion. The excess fair value of the brand is now less than 5% of its carrying value. Processed ingredients found in Lunchables and the high sodium content have not helped the perception of this brand in recent times.

To attach some numbers for context, I used a few assumptions in my exercise.

  1. I based my valuation multiples on full-year revenue for 2024.
  2. I have used conservative enterprise value to sales multiples to make some high-level estimates as these business units have faced impairments, lackluster growth, and are suffering from secular headwinds.
  3. Since I am using EV multiples, it is important to remember that the total proceeds KHC will receive from divestitures will be reduced by the debt tied to specific business units and any transaction costs.

The meat platform reported $2.136 billion in revenue last year. Attaching an EV/Sales multiple of 0.6, the estimated divestiture proceeds come to $1.28 billion.

The coffee platform reported revenue of $835 million in 2024. Attaching an EV/Sales multiple of 0.8 gives us total gross proceeds of $668 million.

The cheese platform reported revenue of $1.746 billion in 2024. This is home to some of Kraft Heinz's iconic assets. Assuming the company divests 40% of this business and attaching an EV/Sales multiple of 0.9, estimated gross proceeds come to $628 million.

For Lunchables, estimating gross proceeds is not straightforward, as the company does not report Lunchables revenue directly. Given that the Substantial Snacking platform had revenue of $1.23 billion last year, I think it is reasonable to estimate Lunchables revenue of somewhere close to $738 million (60% of Substantial Snacking revenue). Attaching an EV/Sales multiple of 1 gives us total gross proceeds of $738 million.

Based on these estimates, Kraft Heinz may be able to bring in gross proceeds of $3.3 billion through the divestiture of underperforming assets. As a reminder, these are purely my estimates based on publicly available information and conservative valuation multiples. My goal is not to act as if I have a crystal ball to predict the future, but to reasonably estimate what kind of numbers we are looking at if Kraft Heinz were to divest some of its underperforming assets.

Kraft Heinz May Enjoy a Valuation Re-Rating Post Transactions

The company carries total debt of around $21.6 billion, which includes both short and long-term debt. Net debt, therefore, comes to around $19.5 billion as KHC had cash and equivalents of just over $2.1 billion as of Q1 2025. If Kraft Heinz were to successfully raise $3.3 billion and use the proceeds for debt repayments, we are looking at the possibility of a reduction in its leverage ratios.

Kraft Heinz reported adjusted EBITDA of around $6.3 billion for 2024. Based on this, the current net leverage comes to 3.1x. In the post-divestiture scenario, I have slashed the company's EBITDA by $500 million to account for divested businesses, which leaves us with an adjusted EBITDA of approximately $5.8 billion. If all gross proceeds were used for debt repayment, the net leverage ratio would therefore come down to around 2.7 from 3.1.

Lower net leverage ratios may attract favorable credit rating actions from rating agencies and may also improve the investor sentiment toward the company. For the past five years, the massive debt burden has limited Kraft Heinz's flexibility to invest in growth avenues. A reduction in leverage, in my opinion, will pave the way for a reversal of this.

Improved portfolio quality, on the other hand, is likely to help the margin profile of the company, again resulting in an improvement in investor sentiment.

Based on these expectations, I believe a valuation re-rating is on the cards for KHC if everything goes to plan.

The key term here is if everything goes to plan.

A lot can still go wrong for the company. The fundamental challenges faced by its core portfolio are unlikely to go away on their own. Kraft Heinz needs to proactively position its portfolio to benefit from secular tailwinds, not swim against the tide.

Takeaway

Kraft Heinz made an important announcement yesterday, confirming an evaluation of strategic transactions to boost long-term shareholder returns. Although the company may not take any actions following this evaluation, I believe asset divestitures are likely in the future. The company may potentially raise more than $3 billion from the divestiture of underperforming assets, paving the way for a meaningful reduction in leverage ratios. In that case, a valuation re-rating will be on the cards. I maintain my hold rating in the wake of this new announcement to evaluate how Kraft Heinz may actively consider strategic transactions.

data.title: May 2025 Active Management Insights - Tariff Risks Reshape Manager Positioning
data.description:
Originally published on May 6, 2025

5 Key Macro Trends

1. Tariffs are driving equity managers’ defensive posture

After entering the year with a cautious outlook, managers have become more defensively postured as the U.S. tariff policy has increased uncertainty. Global managers have been reducing exposure to the U.S. and rotating toward emerging markets. Within the U.S., equity investors are focusing on companies with stable business models and limited exposure to international trade. Managers observe that many companies are delaying spending and investing decisions until there is greater clarity regarding U.S. tariffs.

Outside the U.S., managers in other developed markets are looking to own more domestically focused companies. Most notably, equity investors in Europe and Japan have cited a favorable opportunity for domestic demand-oriented stocks.

2. The AI outlook is shifting

DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) announcement of its open-source, lower-cost AI alternative also caused a significant shift in managers’ outlooks. Investors became more cautious regarding their longer-term expectations for Nvidia (NVDA) as the leader in AI semiconductors. Competition has also increased cost pressure among hyperscalers. Investors have increased conviction on the resilience of global foundry leaders that can weather the impacts through better economies of scale.

Investors continue to view AI as a long-term secular growth opportunity, but are shifting their focus to software companies and other downstream applications that will benefit from AI.

3. China sentiment is improving

Several positive trends are developing within China. In addition to the DeepSeek launch underscoring increased competitiveness in AI, a more constructive stance around technology is emerging from the Chinese government.

Managers remain constructive on the Chinese consumer, noting that previous stimulus measures are beginning to take effect and improving the outlook for consumer companies. The improving environment for both the consumer and technology sectors is also reviving interest in China’s EV market, with investors becoming increasingly bullish on Chinese EV manufacturers.

4. U.S. financial regulation has fueled uncertainty

The outlook for a widely anticipated M&A boom in the U.S. has faded before it got started, as credit spreads have widened around economic uncertainty. This has delayed an expected rally in small caps, which benefit from M&A activity. Investors are also reducing exposure to private equity firms and investment banks.

5. Global economic trends are favoring gold miners and copper

In resource-rich markets - including Australia, Canada and South Africa - gold miners were key beneficiaries of increasing inflation concerns and the supportive price environment. Managers continue to be bullish in the near term as fears of a global slowdown have risen.

In a continuing theme, investors remain optimistic about copper in the long term, as supply is expected to lag demand for multiple years.

Global Equities

Investors seek to navigate tariff disruption

  • Growth managers are reducing exposure to U.S. consumer discretionary companies that depend on global supply chains.
  • Given the slide in U.S. dollar strength, some managers have rotated into emerging markets, including domestic Asian and Latin American banks.

Defensive track to help temper cost pressures

  • Risks of profit contraction saw some value investors shift toward undervalued, durable companies. These include staples and pharmaceuticals that are relatively resilient with healthy balance sheets and stable growth.
  • Both global value and growth managers have increased energy exposure to reduce inflationary tail risks.

DeepSeek raises eyebrows over AI compute spend

  • Investors were caught off-guard by DeepSeek’s trained, low-cost alternative as an AI engine. This has resulted in a re-evaluation of chip exposure, since better AI accessibility has led to an increased focus on software and downstream applications being among the next wave of beneficiaries.
  • Competition has also pushed up cost pressures among hardware hyperscalers. Investors are doubling down on the resilience of global foundry leaders that can weather the impacts through better economies of scale.

Japanese banks on the cusp of a positive earnings cycle

  • In the wake of increasingly persistent inflation, higher anticipated interest rates from the Bank of Japan (BOJ) are expected to boost net interest margins among local Japanese banks.

Cashing in on gold amid market uncertainty

  • Real asset investors are taking profits on gold miners that rallied on the back of historic peak gold prices. Reallocations to other precious metals such as platinum and palladium serve as diversifiers and are expected to benefit from structural demand-supply tailwinds.

U.S. Equities

Reverse engines

  • Relative to the pervasive bullishness at the start of the year, managers generally repositioned portfolios more defensively through the first quarter, as a pullback in mega-cap tech stocks and uncertainty surrounding trade and monetary policies have muddled the outlook for growth and corporate earnings.

Trade policy uncertainty

  • Last quarter, managers expected tariffs to be enacted on an industry-by-industry basis to promote reshoring of manufacturing within the U.S. Instead, the Trump administration’s across-the-board "reciprocal" tariff policy and tit-for-tat trade war with China is now expected to upend supply chains, creating significant uncertainty for both corporate management and equity managers.
  • With a wider range of potential outcomes in policy, managers are positioning portfolios more domestically and defensively, reducing cyclical exposure and adding to sectors unencumbered by tariffs.

Market uncertainty

  • Government policy pronouncements are also creating uncertainty within financial markets, with higher interest rates and credit spreads, and a lack of consensus around margins and valuations undercutting a widely anticipated M&A boom before it started.
  • Managers are reducing exposure to capital markets holdings like private equity firms and investment banks previously expected to benefit - and to financials more broadly - as credit risk increases.

Another false dawn for small caps

  • Small-cap stocks were expected to benefit from the reorientation of production domestically, lowered levels of regulation and increased M&A activity. With those trends in reverse, small cap stocks have again underperformed their large cap peers.
  • While acknowledging the historical cheapness of small caps - both in absolute terms and relative to large caps - managers nonetheless do not expect small caps to outperform until there is more certainty on trade policy and the trajectory of interest rates.

Emerging Markets Equities

China: Positive sentiment despite tariff concerns

  • Tech and innovation: A more constructive stance around technology is emerging from the Chinese government. The launch of DeepSeek underscored China’s competitiveness in the AI landscape and has reignited interest in the tech sector. Cheaper models could further lower costs and allow Chinese companies, particularly those closer to AI monetization - such as internet platforms - to benefit.
  • Consumer rebound: Managers remain constructive on consumer stocks, noting that previous stimulus measures are beginning to filter through, as consumer confidence improves.
  • EV sector interest: Positive momentum in the technology and consumer sectors has also sparked renewed interest in China’s EV market, with some managers increasingly viewing Chinese EV manufacturers as leaders in the industry.

Valuation opportunities in Brazil

  • Brazil has faced significant capital outflows driven by fiscal spending, interest rate policy and political concerns. However, a combination of weak valuations and a potential change in leadership after the 2026 presidential election has renewed investor interest, although uncertainty around timing remains.

Early signs of easing inflation in India

  • The continued underperformance of the Indian market, combined with positive economic signs such as moderating inflation, is starting to attract investors toward domestic opportunities - specifically within the financial sector.

South Africa continues to be a bright spot

  • Managers maintain a positive outlook on South Africa, with investors believing the country could further benefit from a supportive gold and precious metals price environment.

Commodities: Positive views on copper and lithium

  • Many investors remain positive on commodities - particularly copper and lithium due to their structural growth tailwinds, which are supported by anticipated supply-demand imbalances.

Long/Short Equity

Reduced gross and net exposure

  • Global long-short ratios reached five-year lows, reflecting a strong preference for capital preservation and decreased cyclical exposure in consumer and software sectors.

Momentum exposure reduced

  • Hedge funds significantly reduced exposure to high-momentum stocks, marking the largest monthly reduction since 2010.

Selective Asian market adjustments

  • Funds notably reduced exposure to Chinese technology stocks amid renewed regulatory and market volatility concerns, reversing prior bullish sentiment.

Europe and UK Equities

Improving cyclical picture and a German fiscal bazooka

  • Cyclical indicators (PMI, loan growth and unemployment) are improving, and ongoing European Central Bank (ECB) rate cuts should provide further support for growth in Europe. Additionally, the recent German election yielded a positive surprise, as the new centrist coalition agreed on a relaxation of fiscal rules and on a major (EUR 500 billion) fiscal package. Managers expect that this environment will benefit cyclical industries (industrials, materials, banks and utilities, etc.), which remain very cheap relative to history.

Could small caps be the winners?

  • Larger companies have been the primary winners from globalization. However, equity investors see a return to an era of higher trade barriers, or even protectionism, as a tailwind for smaller, domestically oriented companies. These companies will be less impacted by uncertainty around global trade or economic slowdowns in the U.S. and China.

“High quality + low price” is the sweet spot for UK investors

  • The underperformance of UK domestics and mid/small caps are providing stock selection opportunities in market leaders that sold off with their lower-quality peers. Consumer-exposed names in particular have been hit hard on inflation and growth concerns, but the belief is that these environments ultimately lead to the strong getting stronger. Investors are finding opportunities in best-in-class companies that are also the lowest-cost operators and well-positioned to defend margins and volumes - particularly in areas such as food & beverages and clothing retail. Heightened market volatility can offer opportunities to access such names at attractive valuation levels on a medium- to long-term outlook.

Japan Equities

Significant increase in uncertainty due to Trump’s tariffs

  • While many managers remain relatively optimistic about the long-term outlook for banks, some are reducing their exposure in anticipation of a slower pace of policy normalization from the BOJ.
  • In addition, more managers are forecasting a strengthening of the yen against the U.S. dollar, driven by declining confidence in the dollar or a potential narrowing of interest rate differentials. This has led to a shift toward domestic demand-oriented stocks, underpinned by expectations of a recovery in real wage growth.
  • Amid this heightened uncertainty and difficulty in predicting macroeconomic trends, managers are increasingly focusing on stock-specific opportunities.

Rising concern over competition from Chinese manufacturers

  • Japanese manufacturers, especially in the tech and auto sectors, are facing pressure as China intensifies its focus on domestic production. This shift threatens market share and pricing power of foreign firms operating in China’s manufacturing industry.
  • While many investors are scaling back their tech exposure, some value-focused managers are selectively adding to stocks they believe are resilient amid rising competition from Chinese firms, driven by attractive valuations.

Canadian Equities

Reducing cyclicality

  • Investors are favoring more defensive companies with stable demand profiles, such as consumer staples and utilities.
  • At the margin, they are using market volatility and price weakness as an opportunity to rotate from more cyclical sectors - like consumer discretionary and energy - into comparable stocks that offer lower leverage and stronger balance sheets (and, in the case of energy stocks, higher dividends).

Positive outlook for gold and metals

  • Materials was the strongest-performing sector in Q1, driven by a surge in gold prices as volatility linked to tariff uncertainty persisted. Managers are increasing their exposure to gold and precious metals mining royalty companies due to their defensive nature, expecting continued uncertainty around tariffs and inflation in the near term.
  • While gold miners are favored as an inflationary hedge, investors continue to be bullish about the multi-year growth opportunity for copper mining companies.

Opportunities and rotations within financials

  • Managers are using the volatility to add to long-term secular growth names within financials, specifically in wealth management and alternative asset managers.

Opportunities in information technology

  • Although the broader investor outlook for information technology remains cautious, managers are monitoring the sector for potential buying opportunities as valuations become more attractive.

Idiosyncratic defensive stories

  • Against a backdrop of heightened volatility and concerns around stagflation, investors are seeking defensive, idiosyncratic stories - particularly within industrials - that can grow independently of the broader economic environment.

Australian Equities

Attractive defensive stocks

  • Managers are holding companies that they expect will perform better in an economic slowdown. However, what is considered defensive varies. Gold mining stocks are broadly accepted as defensive, as prices move in sync with the gold price.
  • While historically defensive, some managers are concerned with consumer staples, noting increased competition and potential for decline in demand. Meanwhile, for consumer discretionary and industrial companies with low leverage, an ability to maintain margins and quality products are considered attractive.

Assessing tariff impacts

  • Managers are considering the impact that U.S. tariffs will have on companies, including both direct impact as well as secondary impacts of a U.S. economic recession. There are few companies which are expected to be directly impacted, as the majority of Australian equities are either domestically focused or have U.S. exports as a minor portion of revenue. Some companies are expected to benefit - either due to a lower Australian dollar making products cheaper or increased demand from other countries with reciprocal tariffs on U.S. goods. Managers are not actively trading, however, due to uncertainty regarding the tariff endgame.

Preparing for China stimulus

  • Holdings in industrial metals and mining companies, particularly iron ore and copper producers, are being maintained. Managers view the companies as attractive on bottom-up fundamentals and observe that a significant new supply of iron ore will not come on-line until 2026, supporting current-year iron ore prices. Investors expect upside if the Chinese government stimulates to support their economy.

Real Assets

Listed vs. non-listed - Tactical opportunities in listed REITs

  • Daily trading in listed REITs reflects real-time risk and volatility, which currently offers an opportunistic landscape for active REIT managers relative to more stable private asset values.
  • Valuations continue to favor listed REITs.
  • Investors expect transaction volume in private real estate to remain low in 2025, as market participants delay decision-making due to geopolitical and market uncertainty.
  • Managers expect sector performance dispersion to widen.

Listed real estate - Optimistic uncertainty

  • The direct impact of tariffs on REITs is expected to be minimal due to the domestic focus of REITs, but a trade war has potential to restrain capital investment, particularly in the industrial and logistics sectors.
  • Despite macroeconomic uncertainty, rate cuts are expected later in 2025, which should serve as a tailwind to REITs.
  • Managers are most optimistic on senior housing, where an aging population is expected to outpace supply for the next decade.
  • Real estate investors are modestly rotating out of the U.S. and into Europe and the UK, where they’ve found attractive valuations.

Infrastructure - UK turns on the taps

  • Managers have been overweight select UK water utilities due to the expectation that the regulator would allow increased returns to encourage necessary capital expenditure to improve the operation and resilience of the water supply and sewerage networks. This proved correct, with British water regulator Ofwat increasing the allowed returns for the sector in late December 2024. Managers are retaining their holdings due to the defensive nature of the water utilities.
  • Across regions, managers expect the direct impact of increased tariffs imposed by the U.S. to be low, given the domestic and monopolistic focus of most infrastructure sectors.

Disclosures

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.
